narrative:
J. French Hill, Republican of Arkansas’s 2nd congressional district, arrived in Congress in 2015 carrying credentials that read like a Wall Street resume stapled to a government org chart. A former aide to Senator John Tower, Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Treasury under George H.W. Bush, and founder and CEO of Delta Trust & Banking Corp., Hill is a banker who became a congressman — or, depending on your perspective, a congressman who never stopped being a banker[1][2]. When Simmons First National acquired Delta Trust during his first campaign, Hill collected a $1.3 million payout including an $885,000 bonus on his way out the door and into public service[3]. In December 2024, he was named chairman of the House Financial Services Committee, giving a former bank CEO the gavel over the committee that regulates banks[4].

Hill’s tenure as Financial Services chairman has been a masterclass in legislating for one’s former industry. His first major act was sponsoring a Congressional Review Act resolution to overturn the CFPB’s cap on overdraft fees — a rule that would have saved 23 million American households $5 billion per year in what the Biden administration called “junk fees”[5]. Hill rejected that characterization, insisting overdraft charges are a “service that consumers demand”[6]. The resolution passed the House 217-211 and headed to Trump’s desk. He has simultaneously championed banking deregulation, rolled back Dodd-Frank protections, and targeted the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau — the very agency created to protect consumers from predatory banking practices[7]. On the 80% Bill’s Ban Junk Fees article, Hill is not merely absent; he is the opposition.

On other 80% Bill priorities, Hill’s record is similarly bleak. He voted with all House Republicans against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation[8]. He voted to release the Epstein files with the near-unanimous majority in November 2025[9]. But he has not cosponsored legislation on congressional stock trading bans, the DISCLOSE Act, corporate PAC bans, Pentagon audits, paid family leave, or term limits. His Social Security work has focused narrowly on disability insurance reform aimed at getting beneficiaries “back to work,” not on shoring up the program’s long-term funding[10].

The stock trading issue deserves special attention. Hill has made over 30 stock trades totaling more than $900,000 over the past three years, including selling up to $810,000 worth of Blackstone stock in June 2025[11][12]. Blackstone is one of the largest corporate purchasers of single-family homes in America, with a portfolio exceeding 60,000 properties[13]. Hill chairs the committee that regulates Blackstone. He also sold up to $365,000 in Pfizer stock[14]. The 80% Bill calls for banning congressional stock trading; Hill’s portfolio explains why he has not signed on.

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Hill was the top House recipient of Coinbase donations in the 2024 cycle, receiving $48,000 from the crypto exchange’s employees and PAC, including nearly $12,000 from CEO Brian Armstrong personally[15]. Andreessen Horowitz, the crypto venture capital giant, added another $32,500[16]. Hill’s financial sector haul across two campaigns totaled $2.2 million, earning him a spot on the “Wall Street Flunkies” list[17]. His estimated net worth exceeds $7 million[18]. He represents a district centered on Little Rock, where median household income is a fraction of that.

Hill is the 80% Bill’s natural antagonist: a millionaire banker-turned-congressman who chairs the banking committee, trades stocks in companies he regulates, kills consumer protection rules, and collects donations from the industries he oversees. The only question is whether his constituents in AR-2 realize they are being represented by Wall Street, not by Little Rock.

Senator Maggie Hassan arrived in Washington carrying two things: a genuine personal story about disability advocacy inspired by her son Ben, who has cerebral palsy, and an apparently bottomless appetite for lobbyist cash [1]. A former governor who once championed Medicaid expansion in Concord, Hassan has evolved into the Senate's most prolific collector of lobbyist dollars, hauling in over $436,000 in a single cycle from K Street operatives representing Google, AstraZeneca, Blackstone Group, and—despite her public opposition to fossil fuels—$16,750 from lobbyists for Exxon Mobil, BP, and Shell [2][3].

To her credit, Hassan has cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to require dark-money donor transparency [4] and co-introduced legislation to expand Medicare drug price negotiation alongside her fellow Granite State senator, Jeanne Shaheen [5]. She supported the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, and publicly called for changing Senate rules to allow a simple majority vote on democracy protections [6]. She backed a war powers resolution to rein in unchecked executive military authority [7] and cosponsored a foreign lobbying disclosure bill to close loopholes exploited by adversarial governments [8].

But the contradictions pile up like unread ethics filings. Hassan talks about getting 'big money out of politics' while sitting atop the lobbyist-cash leaderboard among rank-and-file senators [2]. She pressed mobile-home park corporations on predatory rent practices [9], a laudable move, yet her own campaign coffers overflow with contributions from the very industries she claims to regulate. Seventy-eight percent of her 2022 donors lived outside New Hampshire, meaning the senator from the Granite State is largely funded by people who have never shoveled a driveway in Manchester [10].

Hassan occupies the uncomfortable space many Democratic senators inhabit: genuinely good positions on paper—drug pricing, voting rights, dark-money disclosure—undercut by a fundraising operation that reads like a K Street phonebook. Whether that tension is hypocrisy or pragmatism depends on whether you ask her consultants or her constituents.

Clay Higgins is the Republican congressman representing Louisiana's 3rd District, a former law enforcement officer who parlayed viral Crime Stoppers videos into a congressional career. Dubbed the "Cajun John Wayne" for his on-camera habit of threatening suspects by name and promising to hunt them down, Higgins resigned from the St. Landry Parish Sheriff's Office in 2016 after the ACLU criticized him for calling Black suspects "animals" and "heathens" [1]. Before that, he resigned from the Opelousas Police Department in 2007 while facing disciplinary action for allegedly using excessive force on a handcuffed Black man and then lying about it [1]. He now serves on the House Homeland Security Committee, the House Oversight Committee -- where he chairs the Subcommittee on Federal Law Enforcement -- and the House Armed Services Committee [2].

On the 80% bills, Higgins's record is a masterclass in populist posturing without populist follow-through. As a Freedom Caucus member, he talks a big game about cutting government waste and has led letters urging topline spending reductions [3], which sounds like it might overlap with auditing the Pentagon -- except he has not championed that specific cause. On the Epstein Files Transparency Act, Higgins achieved the remarkable distinction of being the sole member of the entire House of Representatives to vote against it, 427-1 [4]. His explanation -- that the bill would expose innocent witnesses and abandon "250 years of criminal justice procedure" -- was met with skepticism from sexual trauma organizations who pointed out the bill specifically protects victims, not powerful adults connected to Epstein's crimes [4].

Higgins has not co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, ban corporate PACs, enact the DISCLOSE Act, or impose term limits -- despite serving in Congress since 2017, which is starting to feel like quite a long term itself. On police body cameras, a bill with overwhelming public support, the former officer who twice left law enforcement under clouds of misconduct allegations has been notably silent. He filmed a selfie video inside an Auschwitz gas chamber in 2017, using it to argue for stronger homeland security before the Auschwitz Memorial condemned the footage and he apologized [5]. In 2024, he posted inflammatory comments about Haitian immigrants, calling them "wild" before eventually deleting the post [6].

Follow the money and you find a relatively modest operation fueled by conservative PACs and Louisiana industries. In the 2023-2024 cycle, Higgins received roughly $76,000 from Republican and conservative PACs, $62,000 from crop production and agribusiness, and $47,000 from retirees, with the House Freedom Fund contributing $18,200 [7]. Individual contributions of around $300,000 came largely from local farmers, energy-sector employees, and small business owners. His missed-vote rate of 6.6% is more than three times the congressional median [8], suggesting that showing up is not always his strong suit either.

The bottom line on Clay Higgins is that he is a congressman who built his brand on being tough on crime but who cast the lone vote to keep the Epstein files sealed, who rails against government overreach but chairs a federal law enforcement subcommittee, and who has not lifted a finger for any of the twenty bills that 80% of Americans support. The Cajun John Wayne, it turns out, is shooting blanks.

Senator Cindy Hyde-Smith has represented Mississippi in the U.S. Senate since 2018, when Governor Phil Bryant appointed her to fill Thad Cochran's vacant seat. Before entering politics, Hyde-Smith worked as a lobbyist for the Southern Coalition for Safer Highways and the National Coalition for Healthcare, with offices in Washington, D.C., and San Francisco.[1] She then served in the Mississippi State Senate for twelve years before becoming the state's Commissioner of Agriculture. It is a biography that traces a clean line from lobbying to legislating -- a career arc the 80% Bill was practically designed to interrupt.

On the question of banning congressional stock trading, Hyde-Smith's record is unambiguous. In January 2026, she joined fellow Republicans on the Senate Rules and Administration Committee in a party-line 9-8 vote to table an amendment that would have prohibited senators and their spouses from trading stocks.[2][3] This is notable given that her own financial disclosures show she does not personally trade individual stocks.[4] She voted to preserve a privilege she does not even use -- a gesture of solidarity with colleagues who do. On the Epstein files, Hyde-Smith voted in September 2025 to table an amendment requiring Attorney General Pam Bondi to release the government's Epstein records, reportedly giving a "gleeful thumbs-up" as she did so.[5] She later went along when the Senate unanimously passed the Epstein Files Transparency Act in November 2025, once the political winds made opposition untenable.[6]

Hyde-Smith opposed the For the People Act, which contained provisions addressing gerrymandering, dark money disclosure, and campaign finance reform -- hitting at least four planks of the 80% Bill in a single vote.[7] She objected that the bill would "nullify Mississippi's successful voter ID law," a claim fact-checkers found misleading, and argued against allowing Sunday voting because it falls on "the Christian Sabbath" -- a day Black churches in Mississippi have long used to organize rides to polling places.[8] On Medicare drug negotiation, she cosponsored a Republican alternative, the Prescription Drug Pricing Reduction Act, but voted against the Inflation Reduction Act that actually empowered Medicare to negotiate prices.[9] She did break with her party to support capping insulin at $35 for private insurance patients -- a rare deviation that made headlines precisely because it was so unusual.[10]

The money tells its own story. After Hyde-Smith's 2018 "public hanging" remark -- made in a state with the highest lynching count in American history, while running against Black candidate Mike Espy -- corporate PACs from Walmart, AT&T, Google, Pfizer, Facebook, Major League Baseball, and others publicly demanded refunds of their donations.[11][12] But corporate memory proved short: by her 2020 cycle, a company that had publicly denounced the comments was writing her last-minute checks again.[13] Her top industry contributors over her career have included securities and investment, oil and gas, and the pharmaceutical sector -- the very industries whose regulatory oversight several 80% Bill provisions would strengthen.[14]

The bottom line on Cindy Hyde-Smith is the gap between what Mississippi voters want and what their senator delivers. She has voted against stock trading bans, campaign finance transparency, Medicare drug negotiation, and the release of the Epstein files. She began her career as a lobbyist, and the 80% Bill's lifetime lobbying ban would close the revolving door she once walked through. For a senator who owes her seat to a gubernatorial appointment rather than a competitive primary, the pattern is less about ideology than insulation -- from accountability, from transparency, and from the 80 percent of Americans who support these reforms.

Jahana Hayes came to Congress with a story that practically dared you not to root for her. A former teen mother who grew up in Waterbury public housing, she became the 2016 National Teacher of the Year before flipping Connecticut's 5th District in 2018 [1][2]. She campaigned as the anti-establishment candidate, telling voters she would reject corporate PAC money because "all PACs are not created equal" [3]. Now serving on the Education and Workforce Committee and as ranking member of the Agriculture Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture, Hayes has positioned herself as a voice for working families, students, and rural communities [4]. It is a compelling brand, even if the fine print has not always matched the billboard.

On the merits of the 80% Bill, Hayes has a genuinely strong record on several fronts. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which enabled Medicare to negotiate drug prices and capped insulin costs at $35 a month, and she has called for Medicare for All [5][6]. She co-sponsored and voted for the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which included provisions on body cameras, use-of-force standards, and ending qualified immunity [7]. She voted to release the Epstein files, stating that "those involved in the abuse or cover-up should be held accountable" [8]. She voted for the Build Back Better Act, which included paid family leave provisions and investments in child care [9]. And from her perch on the Agriculture Committee, she has fought for nutrition programs and farm policy that protects family farmers, voting against the Republican Farm Bill in 2026 after Democrats' amendments were rejected [10].

The corporate PAC situation, however, is where the inspirational narrative runs headlong into Washington reality. Hayes pledged in 2018 to reject corporate PAC money. By 2021, she had collected over $100,000 from corporate PACs, including $5,000 from Home Depot, $1,000 each from Voya Financial and Verizon, and contributions from Walmart, Comcast, T-Mobile, and General Dynamics [11][12]. Her campaign explained the reversal by saying she had "come to realize that PAC money in many cases includes voluntary employee contributions" and is "not reserved simply for amorphous, dark money" [13]. That is technically true and politically convenient, the kind of distinction that sounds reasonable in a press release but hollow to the voter who took the original pledge at face value. The NRCC gleefully labeled her a "hypocrite," and for once the attack ad wrote itself [12].

The tension is especially pointed given that several 80% Bill items, including banning corporate PACs, overturning Citizens United, and passing the DISCLOSE Act, are directly about the kind of money Hayes initially renounced and then embraced. She supports the For the People Act and campaign finance reform in principle, but the gap between her 2018 pledge and her subsequent fundraising tells the story of how quickly Washington's gravity bends even the most earnest reformers. Hayes is not a villain in this story. She is something more instructive: a case study in how the system co-opts the people who arrive promising to change it.

Kevin Hern is a man who built a fortune flipping McDonald's franchises, arrived in Congress in 2018, and immediately began trading stocks with the enthusiasm of a day trader who happens to sit on committees that move markets. With an estimated net worth of $110 million — making him one of the fifteen wealthiest members of Congress — Hern is living proof that the American Dream works beautifully, provided you are already in Congress when you start trading [1].

Hern was ranked the seventh most active stock trader in the House between 2020 and 2022 and violated the STOCK Act in 2021 by failing to disclose up to $2.7 million in trades [2]. In 2025, he racked up another round of tardy disclosures covering $4.2 million to $17.6 million in trades, with his office insisting the filings were not actually late — a defense roughly as persuasive as a student claiming the homework was turned in on time because the dog only ate it briefly [3]. Most spectacularly, in December 2025, Hern sold up to $500,000 in UnitedHealth stock while sitting on the House Ways and Means Health Subcommittee — weeks before the Trump administration proposed flat Medicare reimbursement rates that sent UNH crashing over 10 percent [4]. When asked about a bill to ban congressional stock trading, his office offered no comment [5].

On the 80% Bill, Hern is a near-total miss. He chairs the House Republican Policy Committee, the party's fifth-highest leadership role, and has used that perch to champion tax cuts that disproportionately benefit the wealthy — not to close loopholes or fund Social Security [6]. He has shown no interest in banning corporate PACs (he takes their money), auditing the Pentagon, supporting paid family leave, banning dark money, or enacting term limits. He opposed the DISCLOSE Act and has no record of supporting the right to repair, junk fee bans, or protections against corporate home buying.

Hern is now running for the Senate seat being vacated by Markwayne Mullin, which means Oklahoma voters will soon get to decide whether a man who cannot stop trading stocks in industries his committees regulate should be promoted to a body with even more power over those industries. It is, in its way, the most honest campaign pitch in American politics: vote for the man who has already shown you exactly what he will do with the job.

Chrissy Houlahan is a former Air Force captain, Stanford engineer, MIT policy grad, and the kind of centrist Democrat who will vote to censure a fellow party member for yelling "You have no mandate to cut Medicaid!" at the president — and then spend the next six months yelling essentially the same thing at town halls across Chester County. It's the duality of the pragmatic moderate: decorum first, outrage on a delay.

To her credit, Houlahan has genuine legislative wins on issues that matter. She secured 12 weeks of paid parental leave for federal workers in 2020 and for military service members in 2023 — a cause rooted in her own experience as a young Air Force lieutenant who left active duty partly because the military's family leave policies were, to put it charitably, designed by people who had never held a baby. She co-leads a bipartisan, bicameral paid leave working group with Senator Gillibrand and Republican Rep. Stephanie Bice, making her one of the few members of Congress actually doing something about the 80% Bill's paid family leave plank rather than just tweeting about it.

She's also a cosponsor of bipartisan legislation to ban congressional stock trading, aligning with another 80% Bill priority. And from her perch on the Armed Services Committee, she tried to block $150 billion in Pentagon funding, telling colleagues that Defense Department leadership "cannot be trusted with these, our precious resources." That's not quite a full Pentagon audit, but it's closer than most of her colleagues have gotten — especially the ones who treat the defense budget like a religious text that must never be questioned.

On the Big Beautiful Bill, Houlahan voted no both times and has been vocally opposed, calling it "toxic," "absolutely outrageous," and "against the Constitution." She posted that Republicans voted to kick 17 million Americans off health care and millions off food assistance. She then introduced legislation to reverse the SNAP cuts, and held her 102nd town hall to explain to Chester County residents exactly how $1.86 billion in "draconian" cuts to food programs would hit their communities. That's real constituent work — the kind that doesn't make cable news but actually matters.

The gaps, however, are notable. On banning corporate PACs, dark money, the DISCLOSE Act, term limits, right to repair, junk fees, protecting farmland, banning corporate home buying, and taxing the ultra-wealthy, Houlahan's record ranges from thin to nonexistent. She sits on Armed Services and the Intelligence Committee — powerful seats that attract defense industry money — and her campaign finance profile reflects the usual Democratic establishment funding patterns. The Al Green censure vote revealed the core tension: Houlahan genuinely believes in institutional norms and bipartisan process, but that same instinct sometimes leads her to police her own side while the other side rewrites the rules entirely.

Diana Harshbarger is a Republican representing Tennessee's 1st Congressional District since 2021. A licensed pharmacist since 1987 who co-owns Premier Pharmacy with her husband Robert, she ran for Congress on a platform of bringing pharmaceutical manufacturing back from China and fighting for East Tennessee values [1]. She serves on the powerful House Energy and Commerce Committee, where she is Vice Chair of the Subcommittee on Health [2] — a convenient assignment for someone whose professional life and personal fortune are rooted in the pharmaceutical industry.

Harshbarger's relationship with financial transparency has been, to put it charitably, troubled. In August 2021, the Campaign Legal Center filed an ethics complaint after she failed to properly disclose more than 700 stock trades worth between $728,000 and $10.9 million, in violation of the STOCK Act [3]. The trades involved stocks in dozens of companies that lobby the federal government or hold government contracts, including Facebook, Walmart, Apple, Chevron, and defense contractors like Raytheon [4]. Despite this record-setting disclosure failure, Harshbarger has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading. She did, however, sign the U.S. Term Limits pledge [5] — apparently, the one area where she believes in restraining members of Congress.

The China angle deserves its own paragraph. Harshbarger's campaign ads attacked reliance on Chinese pharmaceutical manufacturing, calling it "detrimental to Americans' well being" [6]. What the ads neglected to mention was that her husband Robert had pleaded guilty in 2013 to misbranding drugs purchased from China that were administered to kidney dialysis patients [7]. Diana claimed she had "no role or involvement" in Robert's company, American Inhalation Medication Specialists. State business filings told a different story: she was listed as the company's secretary, a director, and signed annual reports through 2012 [8]. In November 2025, Robert received a presidential pardon from Donald Trump for the conviction [9].

Her initial 2020 campaign was largely self-funded with a $1.4 million personal loan, but PAC money followed once she arrived in Washington. Six of her first eight PAC contributions came from pharmaceutical industry groups [10]. She earned a perfect 100% score from Heritage Action in the 117th Congress [11] and is a member of both the Freedom Caucus and the Republican Study Committee. She voted against certifying electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6, 2021 [12], and opposed the $60 billion Ukraine military aid package in 2024 [13]. On the 80% Bill's core issues — banning dark money, auditing the Pentagon, ending junk fees, guaranteeing paid family leave — her record is a wall of silence, broken only by the sound of stock trades she forgot to disclose.

Josh Hawley is Missouri's junior senator and the Republican Party's most enthusiastic populist — or at least its most enthusiastic populist brand manager. Elected in 2018 after a stint as state Attorney General, Hawley has built a career on railing against corporate elites while carefully choosing which corporate elites to actually cross. He's the senator who raised a fist to January 6th protesters and then sprinted away from them, a metaphor that extends neatly to his legislative record: bold gestures in the front, hasty retreats in the fine print [1].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Hawley deserves genuine credit on several fronts. He authored the PELOSI Act (later renamed the HONEST Act) to Ban Congressional Stock Trading and muscled it through the Senate Homeland Security Committee on an 8-7 vote, defying his own party and drawing fire from President Trump, who called him a "pawn" [2][3]. He introduced the Ending Corporate Influence on Elections Act to reverse Citizens United, which would effectively Ban Corporate PACs from influencing elections — a move so heretical that Mitch McConnell personally warned Republican senators they'd face "incoming" for supporting it [4][5]. On Releasing the Epstein Files, Hawley broke with Senate Republican leadership and voted to force the DOJ to release all unclassified Epstein-related documents, calling for full transparency [6][7]. He's been vocal about Protecting US Farmland, demanding a ban on Chinese ownership of American land [8]. And he's introduced legislation to protect Social Security and Medicare from debt ceiling negotiations, telling his own party to stop using them as bargaining chips [9].

But Hawley's populism has clear boundaries. He voted for the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which slashed taxes for corporations and the ultra-wealthy rather than Taxing the Ultra-Wealthy or Closing Tax Loopholes [10]. He has not championed Auditing the Pentagon, Paid Family Leave legislation, Police Body Cameras, the DISCLOSE Act, or a ban on Junk Fees. His bill to limit corporate election spending was criticized by the Campaign Legal Center as "not a serious effort" because it exempted nonprofit dark money groups — the very entities that spent millions to elect him [11]. And while he talks about term limits, he has not co-sponsored a Constitutional amendment to impose them.

Follow the money and Hawley's populism gets more interesting. Before January 6th prompted corporations to flee, he collected roughly $753,000 from business PACs over his first two cycles [12]. His 2018 campaign was fueled by David Humphreys of Tamko Building Products, who along with family members donated over $2.8 million [13]. The NRA allegedly funneled money through shared vendors in a scheme totaling $35 million across multiple Republican campaigns, with Hawley among the beneficiaries named in a subsequent lawsuit [14]. After corporations cut him off post-January 6th, Hawley rebranded as a small-dollar warrior, raising $1.7 million from grassroots donors in a single quarter — a pivot as smooth as it was convenient [12].

The bottom line on Josh Hawley: he's the rare Republican willing to pick fights with corporate power, and the PELOSI Act and his Citizens United challenge are no small things. But his populism is selective, his dark money history is real, and his voting record on taxes reveals whose side he's ultimately on. Missouri voters should watch what he does, not just what he tweets.

Josh Harder is the Democrat representing California's 9th congressional district, a Central Valley seat anchored by Stockton and the agricultural communities of San Joaquin County. Before entering politics, Harder took the well-worn path of Stanford undergrad to Harvard Business School to a vice presidency at Bessemer Venture Partners, one of Silicon Valley's oldest venture capital firms [1]. He flipped his seat from Republican control in 2018 and has held it since, winning reelection by comfortable margins while branding himself as a pragmatic anti-corruption crusader from farm country [2]. He serves on the Agriculture Committee and the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Harder has some genuine credentials. He was an original co-sponsor of the Ban Corporate PACs Act, legislation he introduced alongside Rep. Max Rose that would prohibit for-profit corporations from funding political campaigns [3]. He was a founding member of the End Corruption Caucus and helped pass provisions cracking down on lobbyist disclosure as part of the For the People Act [4]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the landmark Medicare drug price negotiation provisions he has since championed aggressively, demanding that San Joaquin County patients be included in listening sessions as the program rolled out [5][6]. He has also voted for the Elijah E. Cummings Lower Drug Costs Now Act, which would have cut prescription drug prices by up to 75 percent [7].

But the contradictions are where the story gets rich. Harder's signature legislative cause is banning corporate PACs, yet investigations by Roll Call and Sludge found that he and other "no corporate PAC" Democrats received millions through loopholes in their own pledges [8]. Harder took $4,500 from the Across The Aisle PAC, a shell PAC set up by the campaign treasurer of his own Ban Corporate PACs Act co-sponsor, which itself was funded by corporate money [9]. His campaign account also received nearly $200,000 from leadership PACs of colleagues who freely accept corporate PAC donations, as well as contributions traceable to Goldman Sachs, Blue Cross/Blue Shield, the American Medical Association, and the Crop Insurance Professionals Association -- all routed through trade association PACs and leadership PACs that technically don't carry a corporate name on the tin [8][10]. It is the legislative equivalent of going vegan but eating a cheeseburger if someone else orders it.

On other planks of the 80% Bill -- congressional stock trading bans, term limits, auditing the Pentagon, taxing the ultra-wealthy, paid family leave, the DISCLOSE Act -- Harder's record ranges from silent to absent. Capitol Trades shows he has made stock trades while in office, though the details of those transactions merit their own scrutiny [11]. For someone who built his brand on cleaning up Washington, the gaps are conspicuous. Harder talks a compelling anti-corruption game, and on Medicare drug pricing he has walked the walk. But on the broader suite of reforms that command overwhelming public support, his ambition appears to stop right where his donor relationships begin.
